title: A perfusion protocol for lizards, including a method for brain removal
author: Hoops

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Reliable fixation of nervous tissue is a prerequisite for valid histological investigations. Transcardial perfusion utilizes the circulatory system to distribute fixative throughout the organism quickly and efficiently. The animal, traditionally a mammal, is opened up just below the thoracic cavity, which is then entered through the diaphragm [1]. The heart is exposed, incisions are made into the right atrium and left ventricle, and a specialized, blunt-tipped needle (perfusion needle) is inserted into the left ventricle or the aorta. Fixative is then perfused throughout the animal. This has the added advantage of removing all blood, which may obscure histological features.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Recently, there have been significant advances in our understanding of the neurobiology of squamate reptiles (lizards and snakes), with particular emphasis on lizards [2], [3], [4]. Although perfusion is a commonly used method of fixation in lizard neuroscience, there is no published methodology for perfusing lizards. Lizards differ from mammals in anatomical and physiological ways that affect the perfusion method. Here, I have taken a standard mammalian perfusion protocol and modified it for lizards. This procedure has been tested on fifteen species of agamid and scincid lizards ranging between 3 g and 300 g. All materials necessary for this procedure are listed in Supplementary Materials 1.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 1. Prepare buffered solutions

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 1.1 Prepare prefix perfusate, fixative perfusate and storage buffer (example solutions are listed in Supplementary Materials 2).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 1.2 Prior to perfusion, warm buffers to room temperature. For mammal perfusions, solutions are often warmed to 37 °C (mammalian body temperature), however lizards are ectoterms and as such room temperature is their body temperature.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 2. Prepare apparatus

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Two types of devices are commonly used to perfuse liquids into the circulatory system: those depending on gravity to propel solutions, and those that use a pump system. I use a pump system, which is generally sufficient for protocols such as Nissl staining and immunohistochemistry. However for some purposes, such as electron microscopy, a gravity system may be more appropriate. Furthermore, gravity systems are useful in the field, as they do not require electricity.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 2.1 Run distilled water through the perfusion tubing and perfusion needle to rinse.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 2.2 Draw prefix perfusate into the perfusion tubing. 2 mL/5 g of lizard is sufficient to flush the blood from the circulatory system. Take care to avoid any bubbles forming in the tubing.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 2.3 For a small lizard, the amount of prefix needed is so small that it is possible to store the entire amount in the perfusion tubing. In this case, measure out the required amount of prefix, draw it into the tubing, and mark off the appropriate place on the tubing with a permanent marker. Then continue to fill the tubing with fixative until there is no air left in the tubing. For larger lizards and when using a gravity system the switch from prefix to fixative has to occur during the perfusion, as with mammals. Always take care not to leave any air bubbles in the tubes.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 2.4 Affix the perfusion needle to outlet end of the tubing.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 2.5 Turn on the perfusion pump and adjust the flow rate until there is a weak but even flow of liquid out the end of the perfusion needle (no dripping). Flow rate is heavily dependant on the gauge of the needle and the model of perfusion pump. To find the correct pressure, start with a pressure at which liquid drips from the needle. Increase pressure slowly until the stream flows steadily. I use pressures between 75 and 100 mmHg. If a gravity system is being used instead of a pump system, an appropriate drip rate must be selected and maintained instead of an appropriate pressure.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 3. Anaesthesia

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 3.1 Prepare a syringe with sodium pentobarbital, dose: 100 mg/kg [5]. I do not recommend inhalation anaesthetics, although I have seen them used. Lizards are able to hold their breath for long periods of time and so determining the dose of anaesthetic an animal has inhaled is difficult [5], [6].

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 3.2 Draw an equal volume of lignocaine into the syringe.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 3.3 Administer the anaesthetic to the lizard via intraperitoneal injection. I recommend cooling the lizard to room temperature first by removing all heating elements from the lizard’s enclosure 12 h prior to perfusion. This makes injections easier as cooler lizards are calmer and have lower heart rates.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 3.4 Complete anaesthesia occurs less than 10 min following an injection of anaesthetic. Once the lizard appears unconscious, test the level of anaesthesia by pinching the lizard’s toe sharply. If the animal is sufficiently anaesthetized, it will show no response.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4. Surgery and perfusion

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: I highly recommend using a dissection microscope for all of Sections 4 and 5. Relative to their body size, lizard hearts and brains are smaller than those of mammals, and some steps are quite intricate and delicate [7], [8].

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.1 Place the animal abdomen-up on the dissection pad.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.2 Pin each foot to the dissection pad (Fig. 1A).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.3 Open the abdomino-thoracic cavity, exposing the visceral organs. To do this, cut through the skin, abdominal wall, and peritoneal membrane just below the ribs, at the posterior end of the sternum (Fig. 1B).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.4 There is no barrier between the visceral organs and the lungs (no diaphragm) [9], so immediately start cutting up the two sides of the chest, through skin, abdominal wall and ribs, to the level of the clavicle (Fig. 1C). As with all perfusions, while cutting through the ribs and body wall be careful not to sever any large blood vessels or damage organs such as the lungs, liver and intestines. A good way to do this is to orient the scissors up and away from the lizard while cutting. The pericardium may be attached to the body wall. If the heart does not detach easily, it can be gently pushed down using a small spatula.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.5 Pin back the flap of skin (Fig. 1D). This should provide a clear view of the heart and major blood vessels.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.6 In lizards the pericardium is stronger than in mammals and must be cut away manually. Carefully lift the pericardium away from the heart with forceps to create a tent of empty space and cut the pericardium open with very small scissors. Use two pairs of forceps to pull the pericardium and enlarge the hole, exposing the heart. Cut the heart free of the pericardium by cutting the gubernaculum cordis, which attaches at the base of the ventricle.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.7 Make a small incision in the posterior end of the ventricle (Fig. 2).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.8 Make a small incision in the right atrium (Fig. 2).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.9 Insert the perfusion needle through the incision in the ventricle into the correct aorta (Fig. 2). In mammals, the perfusion needle is inserted into the left ventricle or aorta. This is impossible in lizards because, unlike mammals, lizards have a single ventricle and two aortas (Fig. 2) [10], [11]. The two aortas and the pulmonary artery all emerge as a single trunk from a ventricular structure called the bulbar ring [10], [12]. For brain perfusion, the perfusion needle must be inserted into the right aorta, from which the carotid arteries emerge [10]. The base of the right aorta is obscured, so needle placement can be difficult (Fig. 2). When viewed under a dissection microscope, the tip of a correctly placed perfusion needle is visible through the transparent wall of the right aorta (Fig. 2). To perfuse tissues other than the brain, it may be appropriate to insert the perfusion needle into the left aorta or pulmonary artery.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: It is important that there is no back flow of liquid out of the ventricle, so it is critical to choose a gauge of perfusion needle that fits securely into the aorta. In larger lizard species it may be possible to clamp the needle in the aorta.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.10 Pin the heart in place. Carefully insert a pin through the ventricle, lateral to the perfusion needle and below the level of the bulbar ring. Push the pin down through the lizard’s dorsum and into the dissection pad. Avoid the spinal cord. Pins may also be used to stabilize the perfusion needle.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.11 Turn on the perfusion pump, which should have been set to the correct pressure in step 2.5. Alternatively, if using a gravity system, open the valve.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.12 Allow the perfusion to proceed until about 2.5 mL fixative per gram of lizard has been used.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.13 Turn off the perfusion pump, remove the perfusion needle, and then remove the lizard from the dissection pad.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.14 Remove the head posterior to the tympanic membranes (if visible) and the jaw bone.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.15 Pin the head to the dissection pad (Fig. 3A). Two pins are inserted through the external nares and two more pins through the upper temporal fenestras (Fig. 4).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.16 Cut away the skin and muscle to expose the base of the spinal column (Fig. 3B).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.17 Find the small opening at the base of the skull, where it attaches to the spine. In this space, cut away the membranes covering the brain, which are exposed in this gap (Fig. 3C).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4.18 Remove the head from the dissection pad and place the head in fixative for 24 h at 4 °C.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5. Brain dissection

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.1 Pin the head to the dissection pad. The lizard skull is far more flexible than the mammal skull and the brain is not fully encased in bone [13]. This means that pressure applied to the head of a lizard is more likely to result in damage to the brain. Pinning the head removes the risk of applying manual pressure to the head.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.2 Cut the skin up both sides of the head (Fig. 3D). Cut through the postorbital bone (Fig. 4) and into the eyes.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.3 Lift up the flap of skin and cut it away.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.4 Pull the spine away from the spinal cord by grasping the spine firmly and pulling it straight backwards, away from the head (Fig. 3E and Fig. 4).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.5 Pull the muscle away from the sides of the skull. There is no bone protecting the sides of the brain, so work carefully.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.6 Carefully remove the posterior portion of the skull (Fig. 4).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.7 Carefully remove the top portion of the skull (Fig. 4). The lizard skull is thicker than the mouse skull, and the brain is smaller relative to body size [8], [13]. To break apart and remove the skull I use a corneoscleral punch instead of the more traditional rongueurs (Supplementary Materials 1). I find the corneoscleral punch to be more adept at the delicate task of removing the thick skulls of lizards without damaging small brains. For very large lizard species, rongueurs may be useful.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.8 Carefully remove the bone covering the olfactory tract and the olfactory bulbs (Fig. 4). While the brain sits just posterior to the eyes, the olfactory bulbs rest anterior to the eyes [14]. They are connected to the rest of the brain by a pair of fibre bundles known as the olfactory tracts, which are delicate and prone to tearing. Extreme care should be taken when removing the bone covering the olfactory tracts (the frontal bone) as the olfactory tracts may be attached to it. If the olfactory bulbs are not necessary for study, it is much more efficient to sever the olfactory tracts at their base and skip steps 5.8 and 5.10.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.9 Cut away the dura (Fig. 3G) using the same method as for the pericardium in step 4.6. This membrane may be blackish (Fig. 3F) and is much stronger than in mammals.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.10 Cut the olfactory bulbs away from the vomeronasal and olfactory nerves. Be careful to keep the olfactory tracts intact. This step can be skipped along with step 5.8 if the olfactory bulbs are not necessary for study.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.11 Remove the ear bones by crushing them and then pulling them laterally away from the brain (Fig. 4).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.12 Lift the brain carefully to expose the nerves attached to the brain, including the optic nerves.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.13 Carefully sever the nerves, keeping the scissors well clear of the brain (Fig. 3H).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5.14 Grasp the spinal cord with tweezers and lift the brain from the lizard’s head.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 6. Post-fixation and storage

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 6.1 Place the brain in fixative for 24 h at 4 °C.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 6.2 Wash the brain in phosphate-buffered saline by replacing the solution three times, swirling gently between each exchange.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 6.3 Store the brain in storage buffer at 4 °C. For sectioning, lizard brains can be paraffin-embedded and sectioned, cryosectioned, or vibratome-sectioned using methods identical to those used for mammalian brains [15]. If cryosectioning, sucrose in phosphate-buffered saline is an appropriate cryoprotectant for most protocols.
